title: ML Development Frameworks
concept:
ML development frameworks provide the tools and abstractions that data scientists and ML engineers use to build, train, evaluate, and deploy models. Understanding them is important for testers because the framework code is part of the testable system.

Prominent frameworks include **TensorFlow** (Google), **PyTorch** (Meta), and **scikit-learn** (for classical ML). Each provides:

- **Model architecture definition**: APIs to construct neural network layers, choose activation functions, and wire components together.
- **Training algorithms**: Built-in optimisers (SGD, Adam) and loss functions that drive the backpropagation process.
- **Evaluation utilities**: Functions to measure accuracy, loss curves, confusion matrices, and other metrics during and after training.
- **Deployment pipelines**: Tools to export models (ONNX, TensorFlow SavedModel), serve predictions, and integrate with production systems.
- **GPU acceleration**: Automatic offloading of tensor operations to GPU hardware.
- **Pre-built components**: Layers, regularisers, data loaders, and augmentation tools that teams can reuse.

From a testing perspective, **framework evaluation code must itself be reviewed** — if a model's performance is measured using a misconfigured evaluation loop, the reported metrics will be wrong, leading to false confidence in quality. Testers should also verify that the correct framework version is installed and that GPU configurations match the training environment.
keyPoints: ML frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn) provide model architecture tools, training algorithms, evaluation utilities, and deployment pipelines.
Framework code — including evaluation logic — must itself be reviewed and tested, as errors there can produce misleading performance metrics.
Frameworks abstract low-level mathematics and support GPU acceleration; testers must verify version compatibility and environment consistency.

quiz.question: A data scientist reports that the model achieves 97% accuracy based on the evaluation loop in the training script. A tester later discovers the evaluation loop accidentally tested the model on the training data, not the test data. Which testing principle does this illustrate?
options: The model framework itself is defect-free, so the reported metric must be correct.
Framework evaluation code must be reviewed and tested, as errors in it can produce misleading performance metrics.
Accuracy is always reliable and does not require verification of how it was computed.
GPU acceleration causes numerical errors that inflate accuracy figures.
quiz.explanation: This illustrates that ML framework evaluation code is itself software and subject to defects. A test-on-training-data bug makes 97% accuracy meaningless. Testers must review how evaluation metrics are computed — the measurement instrumentation is part of the testable system.
